data.title: Citigroup's Jane Fraser signals more jobs cuts this year - report
data.description:
Citigroup (C) CEO Jane Fraser signaled that additional layoffs could come this year as the investment bank pushes staff to focus on achieving profitability targets, according to a media report on Wednesday. 

In a memo to her 226K employees, Fraser wrote, "we are not graded on effort," Bloomberg reported. “We are judged on our results. And I expect to see the last vestiges of old, bad habits fall away, and a more disciplined, more confident, winning Citi emerge in 2026.”

The note was sent as the bank posted better-than-expected Q4 earnings, demonstrating solid underlying growth, with particular strength in investment banking and wealth management. It had been reported that Citi (C) was planning to cut about 1K jobs this week as Fraser continues her campaign to streamline the bank and improve returns.

What's more, Citi (C) said that by its own metrics roughly 80% of the programs designed to address its regulatory issues are at or near their target state, and once those efforts are completed, some of the staff hired to meet regulators' rules are likely to be cut.

“Over time, we can expect automation, AI and further process simplification to reshape how work gets done — some roles will change, new ones will emerge and others will no longer be required,” Fraser wrote, as quoted by Bloomberg.

Citigroup (C) didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

data.title: U.S. stocks deep in the red as tech trade suffers, traders digest more inflation data
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages saw another day of declines on Wednesday after the wholesale inflation data came in line with consensus, while retail sales were hotter than expected.

In addition, top technology stocks declined after reports that China told local companies to stop using cybersecurity software made by U.S. companies due to national security concerns.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.8% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1.4%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.3%.

The U.S. Producer Price Index rose in line with consensus in November, while retail sales jumped past consensus for that month.

Moreover, both business inventories and retail inventories in October rose higher than expected, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

“Wednesday's PPI, while delayed, helps to confirm that the inflation data remained stable even during the data drought of the government shutdown and helps to justify the Federal Reserve's rate cut in December, which was initiated largely because of softening jobs data rather than worries about inflation,” said Clark Bellin, president and CIO at Bellwether Wealth.

“Stocks are off to an encouraging start so far in 2026, even with the barrage of political and geopolitical headlines out of Washington. Volatility remains subdued even with a market that is facing seemingly endless headlines, which shows that investors have largely become desensitized to the day-to-day movements out of Washington,” Bellin added.

In addition, the current account deficit narrowed more than expected in Q3 2025. 

Lastly, U.S. existing home sales rose 5.1% MoM to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 4.35M in December vs. 4.230M consensus and 4.14M prior, the National Association of Realtors said.

Over in the bond market, Treasury yields moved lower across the curve, with the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) 4 basis points lower to 4.14%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was 3 basis points lower to 3.52%.

The U.S. Supreme Court was once again expected to release a ruling on the legality of President Donald Trump’s tariffs, but there was no ruling issued.

On earnings, Wells Fargo (WFC) -4.8% reported weaker than expected revenue for the fourth quarter, while Bank of America (BAC) topped Wall Street expectations, still -3.7%.

“We believe the upcoming tech earnings reports at the end of January and early February will be the most important in recent memory. Tech was the best performing sector of 2025 and the guidance that tech companies release in the coming weeks will be important to determine whether or not tech can have another banner year in 2026,” Bellin said.

More on markets

data.title: Alibaba's Qwen press conference could excite, provide growth opportunities: Jefferies
data.description:
Alibaba's (BABA) Qwen artificial intelligence app is set to hold a press conference on Thursday morning that could excite investors, and may even provide new growth opportunities for the tech giant's cloud unit, Jefferies said.

“Based on media interviews [in November 2025], we expect a number of BABA’s applications such as Taobao, Figgy, Taobao Instant Commerce, and Alipay to be integrated with Qwen app,” Jefferies analyst Thomas Chong wrote in a note to clients. “Users can experience a variety of different services under different scenarios.”

Chong, who has a Buy rating and $225 price target on Alibaba, said the company's cloud unit is likely to capture “the majority of incremental industry AI cloud revenue.” And with the rise of AI adoption and AI agents, Alibaba Cloud could grow revenue sharply in 2026.

“Based on our estimates, industry revenue growth is 149% YoY and BABA captures 40% market share in 2025,” Chong explained. “Alibaba Cloud aims to capture 80% of incremental industry revenue growth in 2026. If we assume industry growth can maintain at high double-digit or triple-digit YoY growth in 2026, we expect BABA continues to maintain strong YoY growth momentum at triple digit with market share to reach about 60% this year.”

According to research firm Omdia, AI revenue for cloud computing is expected to increase by 149% year-over-year from RMB20.83B in 2024 to RMB51.8B in 2025. In Alibaba Cloud was first in AI cloud revenue and had roughly 35.8% of the market, more than the second, third and fourth players, combined.

And with Qwen getting more attention (as it provides not only Q&A, but pricing comparison for Taobao, navigation for Amap and various other Alibaba offerings), there is a huge runway for growth. 

“There are about 70% of customers using both its API and GPU services,” Chong posited. “On the other hand, enterprises which have their own datasets use Alibaba Cloud for fine-tuning, pre- and post-training. AI is widely used across different industries, with increasing adoption in various parts of operations. There are still over 90% of enterprises yet to embrace AI opportunities. Examples include: enterprises (auto/financial) use 20–30 years of in-house data training their models; unlock AI potential such as the use of visual large models to measure and identify issues (agriculture); improve productivity in resume screening and tie for interview (recruitment). Limited revenue contribution from [mapping-as-a-service] under AI Cloud revenue. There is significant potential to be unlocked.”

data.title: Crude oil prices to reach $70 in the next days amid geopolitical risks – analyst
data.description:
Citi’s global head of commodities, Max Layton, forecasts Brent crude prices (CO1:COM) could reach $70 in the coming days, with WTI (CL1:COM) climbing to $66-67—a significant rebound from levels below $55.

The rally is being driven primarily by a geopolitical risk premium that has already risen approximately $5 and could increase by “at least another $5,” particularly if military action occurs in Iran or protests continue in the region.

In an interview with CNBC, Layton noted that elevated Russia-Ukraine risks and supply disruptions from multiple countries are adding to the bullish factors pushing prices higher.

“It could potentially all happen very quickly,” Layton said, referring to the speed at which geopolitical developments could impact the market.

Despite the rising risk premium, actual supply disruptions from Iran have yet to materialize. Layton observed that oil production in key regions appears stable with “no obvious signs of disruption at this point.” He noted that in a similar scenario last year, Brent prices surged $15-16 on “no disruption, pretty much all geopolitical risk premium.”

Citi has recently shifted its long-term outlook from bearish to what Layton described as an “above consensus neutral to a very slightly bearish view,” which has actually positioned the firm as one of the more bullish voices on crude oil.

The change reflects significant uncertainty in the market, with wide-ranging estimates on both supply and demand creating what Layton called a “show me moment.”

While a supply surplus has existed, it is being absorbed by factors including China’s stockpiling and ongoing disruptions. “So, between China stockpiling and supply disruption, there isn’t that much of a surplus at the moment,” Layton explained.

However, the strategist cautioned that conditions could reverse rapidly. The geopolitical risk premium “can completely collapse” if there is more certainty around Iran, and Citi’s base case anticipates a potential Russia-Ukraine deal around the summer, which would likely create “pressure for OPEC to cut production again.”

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), (USL)

data.title: Bank of America projects 5%–7% NII growth for 2026 while emphasizing disciplined expense management
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bank of America (BAC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Brian Moynihan, Chairman & CEO, stated that Bank of America reported net income of $7.6 billion for the fourth quarter, up 12% from the same period in 2024, with EPS at $0.98, an 18% increase. He highlighted, "We delivered 7% year-over-year revenue growth. This was led by a 10% improvement in net interest income, up to $15.9 billion on an FTE basis." Moynihan emphasized growth in average loans (8%) and deposits (3%), with operating leverage of 330 basis points in Q4, and described 2025 as a year in which the company "delivered on our commitments to shareholders across the year with solid growth across revenue, earnings and returns." He also noted $113 billion in yearly revenue, a 7% year-over-year increase, and a 13% rise in net income.
  • Moynihan shared that client balances across investments, deposits, and loans surpassed $6.5 trillion, with consumer investment and workplace benefits each crossing $600 billion. He underlined momentum in Global Wealth and Investment Management, and noted that investment banking fees for the full year reached their highest since 2020, up 7% year-over-year.
  • Alastair Borthwick, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Net interest income of $15.9 billion on a fully taxable equivalent basis was strong and a little better than expected, and we saw good momentum from market-based fees that complemented the NII growth." He emphasized expense discipline, noting, "the teams have shown good discipline across the businesses as we held headcount flat across the year despite the volume growth of clients and activity." Borthwick also highlighted a 9% increase in tangible book value per share and $8.4 billion in capital returned to shareholders in the quarter.

Outlook

  • Borthwick reiterated expectations from Investor Day, stating, "At Investor Day in November, we indicated our expectation that we would see 5% to 7% growth in net interest income in 2026 compared to 2025, and that's still our belief today based on the latest interest rate curve, which includes two rate cuts in 2026."
  • For Q1 2026, Borthwick detailed, "we expect Q1 NII will grow roughly 7% from Q1 '25 based on the assumptions on Slide 18, in line with our full year guidance."
  • Management expects about 200 basis points of operating leverage for 2026 and Q1 expenses to be about 4% higher than Q1 2025.

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q4 was $7.6 billion and EPS was $0.98. Revenue for Q4 reached $28.4 billion, with $10.4 billion from sales and trading, investment banking, and asset management fees, which collectively grew 10% year-over-year.
  • Deposits increased by $17 billion from Q3. Tangible book value per share rose to $28.73, a 9% increase from Q4 2024. The CET1 ratio declined to 11.4%, still well above the 10% regulatory minimum.
  • Average loans for Q4 were $1.17 trillion, up $90 billion or 8% year-over-year, led by 12% commercial loan growth. Consumer loans increased by 4%.
  • Noninterest expense for the quarter was $17.4 billion, up less than 4% year-over-year, attributed mainly to incentives and higher trading activity costs. Net charge-offs were $1.3 billion, down about $80 million from Q3, driving the net charge-off ratio to 44 basis points.

Q&A

  • Betsy Graseck, Morgan Stanley, asked about adjusting the expense ratio guidance given accounting changes. Borthwick responded, "I don't think so at this stage, Betsy, but similar to our comments at Investor Day, the numbers that we put out aren't a cap on our ambition."
  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein Autonomous LLP, inquired about expense growth and operating leverage. Borthwick replied, "Ours is an organic growth company. We're investing for growth all the time. And when we perform the way that we believe we can, we're going to create operating leverage every year."
  • Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo, questioned technology and AI spend. Moynihan explained, "Total spending $13 billion plus, $4 billion plus in initiatives, that's all new code... under the 365 Copilot rollout, which is now out across a total of 200,000 teammates and using it and learning from it, we expect to get good leverage of that."
  • John McDonald, Truist, asked about the timeline for CET1 ratio targets. Moynihan answered, "you're going to keep peeling that number down through expansion of our markets business, expansion of lending and other uses of RWA... we'll keep working that down."
  • Matthew O'Connor, Deutsche Bank, discussed loan growth drivers. Borthwick indicated expectations for "loan growth in the mid-single digits" with commercial leading, but consumer picking up.
  • Additional analyst questions centered on expense discipline, deposits, NII growth, loan growth by segment, capital targets, and regulatory changes, with management emphasizing ongoing focus on operating leverage, headcount management, and technology-driven productivity.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly skeptical tone, pressing for clarity on expense guidance, efficiency ratios, and the impact of accounting changes. Multiple analysts pressed for detail on expense management and operating leverage prospects.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, with Borthwick reiterating guidance and Moynihan emphasizing technology investment and discipline: "We have a tendency to actually deliver as opposed to talk about what we do in the future, and that's what we focus on."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was similarly confident, while analysts' questions reflected heightened scrutiny around expense controls and clarity on guidance targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw continued emphasis on operating leverage and disciplined expense management, with explicit projections for 5%–7% NII growth in 2026, compared to a more general outlook last quarter.
  • Management reiterated mid-single-digit loan growth and highlighted ongoing technology investments and headcount stability, in contrast to last quarter's focus on organic growth and capital distribution.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on expense trajectory, efficiency ratios, and capital targets, but current quarter questions showed increased focus on the sustainability and mechanics of operating leverage given the recent accounting change.
  • Key metrics such as net income, EPS, and net interest income all grew from the previous quarter, while tangible book value per share and capital returns were also higher.

Risks and Concerns

  • Moynihan acknowledged ongoing macroeconomic risks: "Risks remain out there. They always do, but we're encouraged and constructive on the year ahead."
  • Borthwick highlighted the impact of the tax equity investment accounting change on CET1, noting it will "come back into our capital over the next several years as those deals wind down."
  • Analysts raised concerns about regulatory uncertainty, headcount management, and the effects of potential rate caps on credit card yields. Moynihan addressed the potential for unintended consequences in regulatory proposals, emphasizing the company’s approach to responsible growth and affordability.

Final Takeaway

Bank of America closed 2025 with strong financial momentum, disciplined expense management, and a clear focus on driving operating leverage and returns. Management reiterated its commitment to 5%–7% net interest income growth for 2026, ongoing technology investments, and prudent capital deployment, positioning the company for continued growth amid a stable credit environment and positive client trends.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Materion surges to all-time high as KeyBanc upgrades on rising demand
data.description:
Materion (MMTRN) +5.3% in Wednesday's trading to an all-time intraday high $148.25 as KeyBanc upgraded the producer of advanced engineered materials to Overweight from Sector Weight with a $161 price target, anticipating multiple expansion from stronger growth and improved margins.

Recent data points within the global semiconductor consumable supply chains reflect a broadening global demand cycle; potential for evermore global defense and space budgets (which could include strategic stockpiling); emerging and likely expanding clean energy business; and ongoing self-help/margin expansion initiatives provide a strong runway for buoyant EPS growth over the next two-plus years vs. the trailing-three-year average of zero, according to KeyBanc's Philip Gibbs.

The analyst believes Materion (MTRN) will further attract cyclical growth investors, while the stock multiple should expand nicely upon successful execution of its growth and margin enhancement initiatives, and any material improvement in precision clad for the global Philip Morris IQOS ILUMA market has become a point of optionality vs. a necessity for the company to grow strongly over the next two years.

data.title: Rising military budgets are reshaping Europe’s defense industry
data.description:
Since Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine in early 2022, Europe’s defense landscape has shifted dramatically as governments across the continent commit to higher military outlays and expanded forces. That has drawn intensified investor attention and driven aerospace and arms stocks to multi-year highs, Reuters reported Wednesday.

EU countries collectively boosted defense budgets to roughly €343 billion ($399 billion) in 2024, about 1.9 % of GDP, with estimates suggesting further growth to €381 billion in 2025, alongside record increases in defense investment, according to EU data.

Allied capitals are raising military spending toward or beyond NATO’s traditional 2 % of GDP guideline, and alliance members have agreed to pursue an even loftier target of 5 % by 2035. Berlin has set up a €100 billion procurement fund and relaxed fiscal rules to fast-track defense outlays, while Poland continues to lead Europe in defense spending relative to economic output.

This strategic emphasis has lifted share prices of major defense manufacturers such as Airbus, BAE Systems (BAESF) (BAESY), Rheinmetall (RNMBF) (RNMBY), Leonardo (FINMF) (FINMY) and Saab (SAABF) (SAABY), and sparked capital markets enthusiasm exemplified by an impending initial public offering from Czech defense group Czechoslovak Group.

Smaller specialist firms are also benefiting as demand grows for sophisticated air-defense, surveillance and electronic-warfare systems, underscoring how geopolitical tensions and renewed security priorities are reshaping Europe’s defense industry.

data.title: Blackstone partners with Empower to bring private markets to 401(k)s
data.description:
Blackstone (BX) is teaming up with retirement services provider Empower to bring private-market investments into the retirement accounts of everyday investors, according to a statement on Wednesday.

Under Empower's private market investment partnership program, participants will be able to invest in Blackstone (BX) funds through collective investment trusts. 

“Partnering with Empower reflects our shared belief that private markets can play an important role in helping more Americans plan for the future and build long-term financial security,” said Blackstone President and Chief Operating Officer Jon Gray. “Bringing Blackstone’s leading investment strategies into defined contribution plans enables retirement savers to access the same opportunities previously only available to institutional investors.”

Empower is the U.S.'s second-largest retirement plan provider by total participants, administering about $2T in assets for over 19M investors. Blackstone (BX), the world's largest alternative asset manager, manages over $1.2T in AUM private equity, credit real estate and infrastructure funds.

The move comes amid growing competition among alternative investment firms to gain access to U.S. 401("k") accounts and other defined-benefit contribution retirement plans. In mid-October, Blackstone (BX) said it launched a business focusing on defined contribution plans.

President Donald Trump in August signed an executive order that aimed to open the door for private equity and other alternatives in 401("k")s.

data.title: High Roller Technologies spikes 688% after prediction market partnership with Crypto.com
data.description:
High Roller Technologies (ROLR) soared in Wednesday afternoon trading after the company announced that it entered into a binding letter of intent with Crypto.com | Derivatives North America for an exclusive partnership to launch an event-based prediction markets product in the U.S.

The events contracts will be offered by Crypto.com's CFTC-registered exchange and clearinghouse and affiliate to customers through HighRoller.com. The partnership will offer people the opportunity to trade event contracts across markets, including finance, entertainment, and sports, through a legal, engaging, and user-friendly platform.

“We’re thrilled to bring High Roller to the USA through this strategic partnership with Crypto.com,” stated High Roller CEO Seth Young. "Pairing the massive appeal of prediction markets with our strong distribution capabilities is an incredibly exciting opportunity, and we’re looking forward to introducing our premium experience to consumers across the country," he added.

The partnership remains subject to the execution of definitive agreements, which will include customary representations, warranties, conditions, covenants, and other provisions. The parties are targeting a product launch in Q1.

High Roller Technologies (HOLR) is a global online gaming operator known for its innovative casino brands, High Roller and Fruta. The company traces its origins to founders Michael Alexander Cribari and Brandon Christopher Eachus, who established the business in 2021 with an original focus on online casino betting. The company went public in October 2024 at an IPO pricing level of $4.00 per share.

Shares of High Roller (HOLR) were up 688% at 2:24 and printed a new all-time high of $33.84 earlier in the session.

data.title: Enterprise Products cut to Sell equivalent at Wolfe after 2025 'free pass' makes stock expensive
data.description:
Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) is little changed in Tuesday's trading even as Wolfe Research downgraded the midstream energy company to Underperform from Peer Perform with a $31 price target, saying a "free pass in 2025" - i.e., a solid year of stock performance despite very weak financial results - has left the stock relatively expensive compared to peers.

Wolfe analyst Keith Stanley believe Enterprise Products (EPD) no longer deserves a premium valuation relative to MLP peers, as the partnership showed the business was not particularly defensive in 2025, the balance sheet is now similar to most peers and less of a competitive advantage, and the incumbent Permian natural gas liquids growth business has become very competitive with overbuild risks.

Stanley also is cautiously optimistic on an eventual recovery in oil markets and relief in Permian gas constraints in 2026 that will favor Permian-focused stocks, but he sees Enterprise Products (EPD) as less likely to see meaningful upside than peers, particularly after solid 2025 stock performance; the analyst also thinks the long awaited step-up in buybacks this year could disappoint given management's typically conservative capital allocation approach.
